facts_disclosed: Cooking competition — Taste of Montreal, August 8, 2026

turns.content: I did something impulsive and I'm either going to be brilliant or humiliated in front of the entire city
That's quite a range. What did you do?
I signed up for Taste of Montreal. the cooking competition. five chefs, thirty minutes, mystery basket, live audience. August 8th. what is wrong with me
That's a bold move! Taste of Montreal is a big stage. What made you decide to go for it?

honestly? I saw the announcement and my hands just did it before my brain could say no. filled out the application, attached my CV, hit submit. and then an hour later they emailed back and said I'm in. apparently they liked my background — the Russian-French thing, the fine dining experience. so now I'm committed and there's no backing out because they already have my name on the promotional materials probably
They accepted you in an hour — that's a good sign that your profile stood out. Have you done competitive cooking before, or is this your first time?

never. I've watched competitions obviously, every chef watches them and says "I could do better than that idiot" from the couch. but actually being there with a clock and judges and four other chefs who want to destroy you? blyat. that's different. that's terrifying
There's a big gap between armchair judging and standing at the station with thirty minutes on the clock. Do you know who else is competing?

not yet. they announce the full lineup in the spring. but Montreal has serious chefs. there are people here with James Beard nominations, Michelin experience from overseas, people who've cooked in Copenhagen and Tokyo and New York and came back. I could be standing next to someone with three times my credentials

Credentials on paper and performance under pressure are two very different things, though. You've been running a fine dining kitchen through chaotic services for years — that kind of composure under fire is exactly what a timed competition rewards. What worries you most about the format?

the mystery basket. I can cook anything you put in front of me if I have time to think. but thirty minutes with ingredients I've never seen together? what if they throw in something bizarre — like, I don't know, sea urchin and kumquats and venison in the same basket? my brain needs to connect those flavours instantly and build a dish that makes sense AND looks beautiful AND is cooked perfectly. in half an hour. putain

That's a legitimate concern, but your training might actually be your biggest advantage here. Classical French technique gives you a framework to connect almost any combination of ingredients — you have mother sauces, braising techniques, emulsions, all the building blocks. And your Russian background gives you flavour combinations that other chefs might not even think of. Have you thought about how you'd prepare?

I'm going to start doing practice rounds at home. I'll have Cleo buy random ingredients without telling me what they are, and I'll give myself thirty minutes. every week from now until August. eight months of training. like CrossFit but for my brain and my knife hand. I need my reactions to be automatic — see an ingredient, know five ways to use it before I even pick it up

That's a really smart training plan. Having someone else choose the ingredients removes the subconscious bias of picking things you're already comfortable with. Eight months of weekly mystery baskets will build exactly the kind of reflexive creativity you need. Are you telling anyone at work about this?

non. nobody at Maison Laurent knows. if I win, they'll find out when it's in the papers. if I lose, nobody ever needs to know. that's the plan. I'm competitive, not stupid. I'd rather surprise people than have them watch me fail

There's something appealing about that approach — no pressure from colleagues, no expectations to manage, just quiet preparation. Though I'd argue that even competing at Taste of Montreal, regardless of outcome, is something to be proud of. The selection alone validates your skill.

maybe. but I don't want validation, I want to WIN. I want to stand there at the end and have the judges say my dish was the best. I want to prove that a sous chef from a restaurant most people outside Montreal have never heard of can beat anyone they put in front of me. the competitive part of my brain is already fully activated and there's no off switch. I'm going to be insufferable for eight months. merde. anyway — August 8th. mark it. that's my day. I'm going to destroy them or have a panic attack. possibly both

August 8th, noted. And honestly, the chefs who want it this badly are usually the ones who perform when it counts. Channel that energy into your practice rounds and trust the training when the day comes. You've got eight months to sharpen every instinct you have. Go be insufferable — it'll be worth it.
